content.problemStatement:
Akash got his money from CodeChef today, so he decided to have dinner outside.

He went to a restaurant having NNN items on the menu. The ithi^{th}ith item on the menu belongs to the category AiA_iAi​ and requires BiB_iBi​ time to be cooked.

Akash wants to have a complete meal. Thus, his meal should have **at least** KKK **distinct** categories of food.

The total time required to get all the food Akash orders, is the **sum** of the cooking time of all the items in the order.

Help Akash find the **minimum time** required to have a complete meal or tell if it is not possible to do so.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains three lines:

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK, denoting the number of dishes on the menu and the number of distinct categories in a complete meal.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers where the ithi^{th}ith integer is AiA_iAi​, denoting the category of the ithi^{th}ith dish in the menu.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers where the ithi^{th}ith integer is BiB_iBi​, denoting the time required to cook the ithi^{th}ith dish in the menu.




Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line, the **minimum time** required to have a complete meal.


If it is impossible to have a complete meal, print −1-1−1 instead.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and K.
- The second line contains N integers A1 … AN, the categories of the dishes.
- The third line contains N integers B1 … BN, the cooking times of the dishes.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum total cooking time needed to obtain at least K distinct categories. If it is impossible, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Akash can choose dish with index 222 having category 222. The total time required to get the complete meal is 111.
Test case 222: Akash can choose dishes with index 3,5,3, 5,3,5, and 777 from the menu.

Dish 333: The dish has category 222 and requires time 000.
Dish 555: The dish has category 444 and requires time 222.
Dish 777: The dish has category 333 and requires time 111.

Thus, there are 333 distinct categories and the total time to get the meal is 0+2+1=30+2+1 = 30+2+1=3. It can be shown that this is the minimum time to get the complete meal.
Test case 333: Akash can choose the only available dish having category 555. The total time required to get the complete meal is 111.
Test case 444: The total number of distinct categories available is 222, which is less than KKK. Thus, it is impossible to have a complete meal.

solution.approach:
For each category we only ever need the cheapest dish, because taking a more expensive dish from the same category never helps to increase the number of distinct categories. Therefore:
1. Scan all dishes and store for each category the minimum cooking time (using a hash map).
2. Collect all these minimum times into a list.
3. If the number of distinct categories is less than K, answer is -1.
4. Otherwise pick the K smallest values from the list and sum them. The K smallest values can be obtained by sorting the list (O(M log M), M = number of distinct categories) or by using nth_element/partial sort for O(M) average time.
The overall complexity per test case is O(N log N) in the worst case (dominated by sorting the distinct‑category minima), and O(N) memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting the distinct minima).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the map of minima.
